sensors.silent_hint: No data received for over 48 hours

sensor_detail.error_description: We can't fetch data for this sensor right now.

explain.empty: The battery is almost empty — replace the sensor soon to avoid missing data.
explain.low: The battery level is very low — schedule a replacement soon.
explain.medium: The battery level is declining — plan a replacement as soon as possible.
explain.high: The battery is fully charged and ready for many months of operation.
explain.empty_no_replace: The battery is almost empty. This sensor type has a built-in battery that cannot be replaced — the sensor needs to be replaced.
explain.low_no_replace: The battery level is very low. This sensor type has a built-in battery that cannot be replaced — plan to replace the sensor.
explain.medium_no_replace: The battery level is declining. This sensor type has a built-in battery that cannot be replaced — plan to replace the sensor in due time.

explain.excellent: The sensor has excellent signal coverage and is sending data without interruption.
explain.good: The sensor has good signal coverage and is operating reliably.
explain.medium: The sensor has medium signal coverage — it works but may experience delays.
explain.poor: The sensor has poor signal coverage and may temporarily lose connection.
explain.none: The sensor has no signal coverage and is not sending data right now.

scenario.label: Scenario
scenario.no_thresholds: No thresholds configured for this scenario

scenarios.sc_open_office_desc: Open office landscapes and large rooms
scenarios.sc_open_office_notes: **Open-plan offices** require balancing multiple parameters. Temperature 22–26 °C (summer) / 20–24 °C (winter) follows EN 16798-1 Cat. II for sedentary work. CO₂ max 800 ppm is critical – concentration and productivity measurably decline above this level. Humidity 35–65 % prevents mucosal dryness and static electricity. VOC max 200 ppb protects against headaches from building materials. Noise limit 45 dB is lower than industry as open offices are sensitive to accumulated background noise.
scenarios.sc_private_office_notes: **Private offices** have a slightly wider temperature range (23–26 °C summer) since individual adjustment is possible. CO₂ and VOC follow the same requirements as open-plan offices for good air quality. Noise limit is set lower (35 dB) as single offices require quiet for focused work.
scenarios.sc_meeting_room_notes: **Meeting rooms** have intermittent high occupancy that quickly raises CO₂ and temperature. CO₂ limit is therefore raised to 1000 ppm (vs. 800 in offices) to avoid constant alarms. Temperature is set lower to compensate for body heat from many attendees. Good ventilation is critical for keeping VOC and CO₂ down.
scenarios.sc_call_center_desc: Call centres and customer service rooms with many simultaneous calls
scenarios.sc_call_center_notes: **Call centres** have constant high occupancy and continuous speech. CO₂ limit set at 1200 ppm and VOC at 250 ppb due to high density. Noise limit raised to 55 dB as speech is an unavoidable part of the work environment. Humidity 35–65 % is important for voice comfort.
scenarios.sc_server_room_desc: Server rooms and technical installation rooms (ASHRAE TC 9.9)
scenarios.sc_server_room_notes: **Server rooms** focus on equipment failure prevention rather than personal comfort. Temperature below 24 °C and humidity 30–55 % follow ASHRAE TC 9.9, preventing overheating, static discharge, and condensation. Higher noise tolerance (60 dB) due to cooling fans. CO₂ and VOC are monitored for occasional staff visits.
scenarios.sc_school_notes: **Schools** have temperature 20–26 °C per EN 16798-1 and national building codes (BR18 in DK / BBR in SE / ASR A3.5 in DE). CO₂ limit of 1000 ppm is central – research shows student learning deteriorates above this level. Low noise (40 dB) supports concentration. VOC max 250 ppb protects against off-gassing from furniture.
scenarios.sc_daycare_notes: **Daycare centres** have stricter CO₂ (800 ppm) and VOC (200 ppb) limits as young children are more vulnerable and have higher ventilation needs per kg body weight. Temperature is slightly lower as children are more physically active. Noise limit (45 dB) accounts for an active play environment.
scenarios.sc_auditorium_desc: Large lecture halls and auditoriums with many listeners
scenarios.sc_auditorium_notes: **Auditoriums and lecture halls** have CO₂ limit raised to 1200 ppm as intermittent very high occupancy makes lower limits unrealistic without massive ventilation. Temperature is lower in winter to compensate for body heat from many attendees. Noise max 45 dB ensures lectures can be heard clearly.
scenarios.sc_sports_hall_desc: Sports halls, gymnasiums and institutional fitness rooms
scenarios.sc_sports_hall_notes: **Sports halls** have lower temperature (16–20 °C winter) as physical activity significantly raises body temperature. Higher humidity tolerance (up to 70 % summer) and CO₂ limit (1200 ppm) as intense exercise produces more sweat, heat, and exhalation. Noise limit is higher (60 dB) due to activity sounds.
scenarios.sc_hospital_notes: **Hospital wards** require enhanced air quality: CO₂ max 600 ppm and VOC max 100 ppb to protect immunocompromised patients. Temperature is slightly higher (23–26 °C summer) for patient comfort. Humidity 35–60 % prevents mucosal dryness. Low noise (35 dB) promotes healing.
scenarios.sc_laboratory_notes: **Laboratories** require a narrow temperature range (20–22 °C) for reproducible results. VOC limit is very low (100 ppb) to avoid sample contamination. Humidity 35–55 % prevents static electricity on sensitive equipment. Noise max 40 dB supports focused work.
scenarios.sc_care_home_desc: Care homes, elderly housing and rehabilitation centres
scenarios.sc_care_home_notes: Elderly residents in **care homes** have reduced thermoregulation and prefer warmer surroundings (22–27 °C). CO₂ max 800 ppm and VOC max 150 ppb are important as residents stay here around the clock. Humidity 35–60 % prevents skin and mucosal dryness. Low noise (35 dB) promotes rest.
scenarios.sc_dental_clinic_desc: Dental and medical clinics, health centres
scenarios.sc_dental_clinic_notes: **Dental clinics** have similar requirements to hospitals. Low VOC limit (100 ppb) due to chemicals such as composites and disinfectants used in treatment. Temperature 21–26 °C and humidity 35–60 % ensure comfort for patient and practitioner. CO₂ max 800 ppm supports good air quality.
scenarios.sc_historic_church_desc: Historic churches with preservation requirements for interiors
scenarios.sc_historic_church_notes: **Historic churches** in Scandinavia have high thermal inertia and should not be overheated. Temperature 7–18 °C (summer) accommodates the building's natural climate fluctuations. Humidity 50–65 % is critical – too low damages wooden furnishings and organs, too high creates mould risk. Seasonal variation reflects the building's natural annual rhythm.
scenarios.sc_modern_church_desc: Modern church buildings without preservation needs
scenarios.sc_modern_church_notes: **Modern churches** tolerate higher temperatures (17–25 °C) than historic ones as newer building materials are more robust. Humidity 45–65 % is controlled to protect art, furnishings, and musical instruments. CO₂ max 1000 ppm ensures comfort during services with many attendees.
scenarios.sc_museum_exhibition_desc: Exhibition rooms with art, paintings and artefacts
scenarios.sc_museum_exhibition_notes: **Museum exhibitions** balance visitor comfort with preservation. Narrow humidity range (47–57 %) protects objects against dimensional changes and chemical degradation. CO₂ max 800 ppm and VOC max 150 ppb are tightened to protect sensitive materials from airborne pollutants.
scenarios.sc_museum_storage_desc: Storage and preservation rooms for collections
scenarios.sc_museum_storage_notes: **Museum storage** follows ISO recommendations for long-term preservation: 12–18 °C slows chemical degradation, and 40–55 % RH prevents both desiccation and mould. Very low VOC (100 ppb) protects against off-gassing from packaging and building materials.
scenarios.sc_archive_notes: **Archives** follow ISO 11799 for paper-based collections. Temperature 14–18 °C and humidity 35–50 % minimise paper's chemical ageing (hydrolysis and oxidation) and prevent mould. Low VOC (100 ppb) protects against acid attack on cellulose.
scenarios.sc_cold_art_storage_desc: Cold storage for fine art, photographic materials and sensitive objects
scenarios.sc_cold_art_storage_notes: **Cold art storage** maintains 10–16 °C and 40–50 % RH for photographic materials, film, and sensitive art. Very low VOC (50 ppb) is crucial as chemical off-gassing can cause irreversible damage to sensitive surfaces.
scenarios.sc_historic_manor_desc: Listed buildings, manor houses and castles with mixed use
scenarios.sc_historic_manor_notes: **Historic manors** have a wide temperature range accommodating mixed use (residence, museum, events). Seasonal variation prevents overheating in winter, which can cause cracks in woodwork and plaster. Humidity 45–65 % protects interiors.
scenarios.sc_residential_notes: Standard **residential comfort** with temperature 23–27 °C (summer) / 20–24 °C (winter). CO₂ below 800 ppm ensures good air quality in living spaces. Humidity 35–65 % prevents mucosal irritation. VOC max 200 ppb protects against off-gassing from furniture and building materials.
scenarios.sc_bedroom_desc: Bedrooms and rest rooms — optimal sleep temperature
scenarios.sc_bedroom_notes: **Bedrooms** follow sleep research recommendations: 15–19 °C for optimal sleep quality. CO₂ limit tightened to 600 ppm as good nighttime air quality is vital for recovery – many bedrooms are poorly ventilated. Very low noise (25 dB) and VOC max 150 ppb ensure undisturbed sleep.
scenarios.sc_childrens_room_desc: Children's and youth rooms with stricter air quality requirements
scenarios.sc_childrens_room_notes: **Children's rooms** are slightly warmer (18–24 °C) than adult bedrooms for comfort. CO₂ max 600 ppm and VOC max 150 ppb are tightened to protect children's health and development – children inhale more air per kg body weight. Low noise (30 dB) supports sleep quality.
scenarios.sc_bathroom_desc: Bathrooms and wet rooms — monitoring high humidity
scenarios.sc_bathroom_notes: **Bathrooms** have higher temperature (22–28 °C) for bathing comfort. Wider humidity range (40–75 %), but important to monitor as sustained humidity above 70 % significantly increases mould risk. VOC is monitored due to cleaning agents and sealants.
scenarios.sc_basement_desc: Basements and storage rooms — focus on mould prevention
scenarios.sc_basement_notes: **Basements** have a wide temperature range (12–20 °C) as they are rarely fully climate-controlled. The most important parameter is humidity – strict limit of max 55 % RH prevents mould, musty odours, and material damage. CO₂ and VOC are monitored to detect poor ventilation.
scenarios.sc_summer_house_desc: Summer house — wide thresholds covering both closed winterised houses and occupied stays year-round
scenarios.sc_summer_house_notes: **Summer houses** have a very wide range (5–28 °C) due to long periods without heating. The most important parameter is humidity – monitor to detect moisture damage, mould, and condensation during unoccupied periods. CO₂ and VOC can reveal lack of ventilation.
scenarios.sc_retail_notes: **Retail stores** have standard comfort with seasonal variation. CO₂ max 1000 ppm for reasonable indoor climate with varying customer occupancy. Humidity 35–65 % ensures comfort for customers and staff. Higher noise tolerance (50 dB) than offices due to customer activity and background music.
scenarios.sc_restaurant_desc: Restaurants, cafés and canteens with cooking
scenarios.sc_restaurant_notes: **Restaurants** have higher VOC (300 ppb) and CO₂ (1200 ppm) due to cooking, many guests, and open flames. Humidity is controlled to limit condensation and odour issues. Noise level 55 dB reflects normal conversation – important for the dining experience.
scenarios.sc_warehouse_desc: Warehouse buildings, logistics centres and depots
scenarios.sc_warehouse_notes: **Warehouses** have wide tolerances (12–25 °C) as personal comfort is secondary to goods protection. Focus is on humidity (30–60 %), which protects stored goods against moisture, corrosion, and mould. Higher noise tolerance (60 dB) due to forklifts and machinery.
scenarios.sc_fitness_desc: Fitness centres, swimming pools and training facilities
scenarios.sc_fitness_notes: **Fitness centres** have lower temperature (17–23 °C) than offices due to intense physical activity. Higher CO₂ tolerance (1200 ppm) and humidity (up to 70 %) as exercise increases exhalation and perspiration. Noise up to 65 dB reflects music and activity. Good ventilation is critical.
scenarios.sc_food_cold_dk_notes: **Danish cold storage** per Fødevarestyrelsen (DK) / Livsmedelsverket (SE) / LMHV (DE): hard limit ≤5 °C. Green zone 0–4 °C is recommended operating temperature; yellow 4–5 °C warns before violation.
scenarios.sc_food_cold_de_notes: **German cold storage** per LMHV (Lebensmittelhygieneverordnung): max 7 °C for general chilled goods. Green zone 0–5 °C; yellow 5–7 °C as early warning. Compare: DK ≤5 °C, SE ≤4/8 °C.
scenarios.sc_food_cold_se_notes: **Swedish cold storage** per Livsmedelsverket: recommendation ≤4 °C (green), legal limit ≤8 °C. Yellow zone 4–8 °C exceeds recommendation but within legal limit. Compare: DK ≤5 °C, DE ≤7 °C.
scenarios.sc_food_cold_uk_notes: **British cold storage** per FSA (Food Standards Agency): legal limit ≤8 °C, recommendation ≤5 °C. Green 0–5 °C (recommended); yellow 5–8 °C (legal but not recommended).
scenarios.sc_food_frozen_eu_desc: EU Regulation 37/2005 & UK: ≤-18 °C for frozen food
scenarios.sc_food_frozen_eu_notes: **EU frozen storage** per EU Regulation 37/2005: frozen goods ≤−18 °C. Green −24 to −19 °C provides safety margin. Yellow −19 to −18 °C is at the limit.
scenarios.sc_medicine_room_notes: **Room temperature medicine** per European Pharmacopoeia (Ph.Eur.): 15–25 °C. Yellow zone at 13–15 °C and 25–27 °C provides early warning. Humidity should be kept below 60 % to protect packaging and tablets.
scenarios.sc_medicine_cool_notes: **Cool medicine storage** per Ph.Eur.: 8–15 °C. Yellow below 8 °C warns of freezing risk; above 15 °C of overheating. Continuous temperature logging is legally required.
scenarios.sc_medicine_fridge_notes: **Medicine refrigerator** per Ph.Eur.: 2–8 °C. Yellow below 2 °C warns of freezing risk (can destroy vaccines and biological products). Red above 10 °C is critical. Continuous monitoring is legally required.
scenarios.sc_production_floor_desc: Light industry, sedentary/standing work. Temp 18–26 °C, RH 30–65 % (EN ISO 7730).
scenarios.sc_production_floor_notes: **Production floors** follow EN ISO 7730 for light industrial work: 18–26 °C. Higher noise tolerance (70 dB) than offices due to machinery – but still below occupational limits (Arbejdstilsynet in DK / AFS 2005:16 in SE / LärmVibrationsArbSchV in DE). VOC 300 ppb allows light industrial emissions. Humidity 30–65 % prevents static electricity.
scenarios.sc_clean_room_desc: ISO 14644 / GMP clean room. Tightly controlled: 20–22 °C, RH 45–55 %.
scenarios.sc_clean_room_notes: **Clean rooms** follow ISO 14644 / GMP with narrow range: 20–22 °C ±2 and 45–55 % ±5 RH. Strict air quality (CO₂ max 800 ppm, VOC max 100 ppb) protects particle-sensitive production. Even small deviations can significantly affect product quality.
scenarios.sc_pharma_production_desc: GMP Annex 1 / WHO: 18–24 °C, RH 40–60 %. Critical for product quality and shelf life.
scenarios.sc_pharma_production_notes: **Pharmaceutical production** follows GMP Annex 1 / WHO: 18–24 °C and 40–60 % RH. Controlled climate ensures product quality and stability. Low VOC (150 ppb) protects against contamination of active substances. CO₂ max 900 ppm monitors ventilation effectiveness.
scenarios.sc_printing_desc: Paper curl and static electricity are primary risks. 20–23 °C, RH 45–55 %.
scenarios.sc_printing_notes: **Print shops** require precise climate control: paper curls at low humidity and sticks at high humidity. Optimal 20–23 °C and 45–55 % RH. Higher VOC tolerance (300 ppb) due to printing inks and solvents. Noise up to 75 dB from printing presses.
scenarios.sc_woodworking_desc: Wood EMC controlled by RH. 15–20 °C, RH 40–60 % gives approx. 8–11% EMC.
scenarios.sc_woodworking_notes: **Woodworking shops** require humidity 40–60 % to maintain wood equilibrium moisture content (EMC) at 8–11 %, preventing cracks, warping, and shrinkage. Seasonal variation allows wider range in summer. Higher VOC (400 ppb) due to varnish, glue, and wood dust.
scenarios.sc_textile_desc: Spinning and weaving require high RH 55–75 % to prevent static electricity and thread breakage.
scenarios.sc_textile_notes: **Textile production** requires high humidity (55–75 %) to prevent static electricity and thread breakage during spinning and weaving. Temperature 20–26 °C. Higher noise (80 dB) and VOC tolerance (400 ppb) reflect the production environment.
scenarios.sc_electronics_assembly_desc: ESD-sensitive processes. ANSI/ESD S20.20: 20–25 °C, RH 40–60 % to prevent electrostatic discharge.
scenarios.sc_electronics_assembly_notes: **Electronics assembly** is ESD-sensitive per ANSI/ESD S20.20: humidity 40–60 % prevents static discharge that can destroy components. Temperature 20–25 °C for precision work. Low VOC (150 ppb) protects against PCB and solder joint corrosion.
scenarios.sc_cold_storage_industrial_desc: General industrial cold store: 2–8 °C. Condensation risk monitored at high RH.
scenarios.sc_cold_storage_industrial_notes: **Industrial cold storage**: 2–8 °C. Monitor humidity for condensation risk above 80 % RH, which can cause ice formation and goods damage.
scenarios.sc_refrigerated_transport_desc: EU Reg. 37/2005 / ATP: fresh produce 0–4 °C. Yellow at ±2 °C deviation.
scenarios.sc_refrigerated_transport_notes: **Refrigerated transport** per EU Reg. 37/2005 / ATP agreement: fresh goods 0–4 °C. Yellow zone at 4–6 °C provides margin for brief temperature excursions during door openings.
scenarios.sc_frozen_transport_desc: ATP: deep frozen ≤ −18 °C. Yellow zone −18 to −15, red above −15 °C.
scenarios.sc_frozen_transport_notes: **Frozen transport** per ATP: deep-frozen goods ≤−18 °C. Yellow −18 to −15 °C marks temporary exceedance permitted during loading/unloading.
scenarios.sc_cargo_container_desc: ISO 1496 general cargo. Dew point risk (condensation) is primary concern during temperature changes.
scenarios.sc_cargo_container_notes: **Cargo containers** per ISO 1496: wide range for general cargo. Condensation risk (dew point) is the primary concern — monitor humidity closely, especially with day/night temperature swings.
scenarios.sc_vehicle_storage_desc: Prevention of corrosion and condensation. 5–15 °C, RH 40–60 % recommended.
scenarios.sc_vehicle_storage_notes: **Vehicle storage** focuses on preventing corrosion and condensation: humidity 40–60 %. Wide temperature range as storage is rarely actively climate-controlled. Monitor RH to protect paintwork and electronics.
scenarios.sc_airport_cargo_desc: IATA CEIV: general air cargo terminal. 15–25 °C, RH 30–70 %.
scenarios.sc_airport_cargo_notes: **Airport cargo terminals** per IATA CEIV: 15–25 °C and 30–70 % RH. Wider tolerances than cold chain but monitoring is important to detect temperature deviations quickly.
scenarios.sc_pharma_transport_desc: GDP / WHO TRS 961: controlled room temperature (CRT). 15–25 °C, RH 35–75 %.
scenarios.sc_pharma_transport_notes: **Pharmaceutical transport** per GDP guidelines / WHO TRS 961: controlled room temperature 15–25 °C. Yellow zone 13–15 °C and 25–27 °C provides early warning. Humidity 35–75 % protects packaging.
scenarios.sc_flower_transport_desc: VBN / FloraHolland: cut flowers 2–5 °C, RH 90–95 % to prevent wilting.
scenarios.sc_flower_transport_notes: **Flower transport** per VBN / FloraHolland: cut flowers 2–5 °C with very high humidity (85–95 %) to prevent dehydration and wilting.
scenarios.sc_boat_winter_storage_desc: Indoor winter storage for boats. 0–12 °C, RH below 65 % to avoid corrosion and mould.
scenarios.sc_boat_winter_storage_notes: **Winter boat storage** typically takes place in heated or unheated boat halls. The primary risks are condensation on cold engine and metal parts, corrosion, and mould on upholstery and covers. Marine associations and insurers recommend 0–10 °C and RH below 65 %. Unheated halls may dip below freezing — acceptable as long as RH stays low enough that condensation does not form on cold surfaces. VOC levels may be elevated due to antifouling paint and fuel vapours.
scenarios.sc_co_working_desc: Shared workspace with varying occupancy. Wider CO₂ tolerance. EN 16798-1 Cat. II.
scenarios.sc_co_working_notes: **Co-working spaces** are similar to open-plan offices with slightly higher CO₂ tolerance (1000 ppm) due to varying occupancy. Humidity 30–65 % and VOC max 200 ppb follow standard office requirements. Noise max 50 dB accounts for conversations and phone calls.
scenarios.sc_reception_lobby_desc: Short stay, wider temperature margin. Door drafts affect the climate.
scenarios.sc_reception_lobby_notes: **Receptions and lobbies** have wider temperature range due to frequent door opening and drafts. CO₂ limit is raised (1200 ppm) as exterior doors cause unpredictable ventilation. Short stays make slightly wider limits acceptable.
scenarios.sc_hot_desk_desc: Flexible desk arrangement. Similar to open-plan with slightly wider range.
scenarios.sc_hot_desk_notes: **Flexible desks** (hot-desking) have the same basic requirements as open-plan offices. CO₂ 1000 ppm for fluctuating occupancy. Noise max 50 dB and VOC max 200 ppb follow standard office requirements.
scenarios.sc_university_lab_desc: Tighter climate control for experimental work. 20–24 °C, RH 40–60 %.
scenarios.sc_university_lab_notes: **University laboratories** have a narrow temperature range (20–24 °C) for precise experiments. Humidity 40–60 % prevents static electricity and protects sensitive equipment. VOC max 200 ppb avoids interference with experiments.
scenarios.sc_library_desc: Paper preservation + comfort. BS 5454 / PD 5454: 16–19 °C, RH 45–60 %. Low noise.
scenarios.sc_library_notes: **Libraries** balance user comfort with paper preservation per BS 5454 / PD 5454. Temperature 18–24 °C and humidity 45–60 % protect books and documents. Very low noise limit (35 dB) ensures a study environment. VOC max 150 ppb protects pages.
scenarios.sc_operating_room_desc: HTM 03-01 / DS 2451-12: 19–24 °C, RH 30–60 %. Stringent ventilation and cleanliness.
scenarios.sc_operating_room_notes: **Operating rooms** follow HTM 03-01 (UK) / DS 2451-12 (DK) / DIN 1946-4 (DE) / SS-EN 16244 (SE). Temperature 19–24 °C balances surgeon's needs (cool) with patient's (warm). Strict CO₂ (800 ppm) and VOC (100 ppb) for sterile air quality. Humidity 30–60 % prevents tissue desiccation.
scenarios.sc_pharmacy_desc: EU GDP / Ph.Eur: controlled room temperature 15–25 °C, RH < 60 %.
scenarios.sc_pharmacy_notes: **Pharmacies** follow EU GDP / Ph.Eur.: controlled room temperature 15–25 °C. Humidity below 60 % protects medication from moisture damage and clumping. VOC max 150 ppb minimises chemical interaction with medicines.
scenarios.sc_gothic_cathedral_desc: Large stone mass with high thermal inertia. EN 15757 adaptive. Stability over absolute value. 8–18 °C, RH 45–75 %.
scenarios.sc_gothic_cathedral_notes: **Gothic cathedrals** have massive stone walls with high thermal inertia – climate should follow the building's natural annual rhythm per EN 15757 adaptive approach. Humidity 45–70 % balances stone preservation with prevention of salt efflorescence and biological growth. Large volumes naturally allow higher CO₂ tolerance.
scenarios.sc_romanesque_church_desc: Thick stone walls, small windows. Lime paintings and frescoes are extremely RH-sensitive. RH 45–65 % critical.
scenarios.sc_romanesque_church_notes: **Romanesque churches** have a narrower humidity range (45–65 %) than gothic ones, as lime paintings and frescoes are extremely sensitive to RH fluctuations. Even brief swings can cause flaking. Temperature should change slowly following the thermally heavy construction.
scenarios.sc_timber_frame_church_desc: Wood expands/contracts with RH. Avoid RH < 40 % (cracking) and > 70 % (mould). EMC 8–14 %.
scenarios.sc_timber_frame_church_notes: **Timber-frame churches** have wooden structures requiring 40–65 % RH to maintain equilibrium moisture at 8–14 %. Below 40 % risks cracking in beams and panels; above 70 % risks mould and wood decay. Temperature should follow seasons to avoid rapid RH swings.
scenarios.sc_castle_museum_desc: Mixed materials (stone, wood, textiles, leather, metal). EN 15757 adaptive. Prevent condensation and metal corrosion.
scenarios.sc_castle_museum_notes: **Castle museums** house mixed materials (stone, wood, textiles, leather, metal) requiring a compromise climate. EN 15757 adaptive approach is recommended – avoid condensation on stone and metal corrosion. Humidity 40–65 % and slow temperature changes are key.
scenarios.sc_palace_residence_desc: Paintings, gilding, tapestries, furniture. ASHRAE Class B / Bizot Group: 18–22 °C, RH 40–55 %.
scenarios.sc_palace_residence_notes: **Palaces and stately homes** with art collections follow ASHRAE Class B / Bizot Group: 18–22 °C and 40–55 % RH. Stricter than churches due to paintings, gilding, tapestries, and polished furniture that are very sensitive to RH fluctuations.
scenarios.sc_wine_cellar_historic_desc: Stable underground temp 10–14 °C, high RH 60–80 % for cork. Also monastery cellars.
scenarios.sc_wine_cellar_historic_notes: **Historic wine cellars** maintain 10–14 °C, ideal for wine storage. High humidity (60–80 %) is necessary to keep corks moist and prevent oxidation. Also applies to monastery cellars and similar underground storage.
scenarios.sc_crypt_underground_desc: Naturally stable, cool, damp. Goal: prevent condensation. 6–12 °C, RH 60–85 %. Salt crystallisation risk.
scenarios.sc_crypt_underground_notes: **Crypts and underground rooms** have a naturally stable, cool, and humid climate. Primary focus is avoiding condensation and salt crystallisation caused by RH fluctuations. Temperature remains naturally low (6–14 °C) and rarely needs active control.
scenarios.sc_bizot_green_desc: Bizot Green standard for hygroscopic collections: 16–25 °C, RH 40–60 %, max. ±10 % RH/24h
scenarios.sc_bizot_green_notes: **Bizot Green Protocol 2023** is an international museum standard for climate control of hygroscopic materials (wood, paper, textiles, leather). Temp. 16–25 °C, relative humidity 40–60 %, and max. ±10 % RH fluctuation per 24 hours. Can be tightened for particularly sensitive collections.
scenarios.sc_attic_room_desc: Extreme thermal variation, condensation risk. Wider temperature range necessary.
scenarios.sc_attic_room_notes: **Attic rooms** have wider temperature range (20–28 °C summer) due to extreme thermal variation under the roof. In winter, condensation risk is high – monitor humidity closely. Good insulation and ventilation are essential to keep climate within limits.
scenarios.sc_wine_cellar_private_desc: Ideal: 11–14 °C, RH 60–75 %. Stability is more important than exact temperature.
scenarios.sc_wine_cellar_private_notes: **Private wine cellars** maintain 11–14 °C and 60–75 % RH. Stability matters more than absolute values – avoid daily and seasonal swings exceeding ±2 °C, as temperature shock damages wine.
scenarios.sc_shed_insulated_desc: Insulated shed or outbuilding — dampened temperature swings. Focus on preventing moisture and mould in tools and equipment.
scenarios.sc_shed_insulated_notes: **Insulated sheds and outbuildings** have significantly dampened temperature swings compared to outdoor conditions but are rarely actively heated. Summer 12–28 °C and winter −5 to 18 °C cover typical Nordic conditions. Humidity should be kept below 70 % to prevent mould on garden tools, bikes, and hobby equipment. Monitor especially in spring and autumn, when overnight cooling increases condensation risk.
scenarios.sc_shed_uninsulated_desc: Uninsulated shed or outbuilding — tracks ambient outdoor temperature. Wide limits, focus on condensation and mould prevention.
scenarios.sc_shed_uninsulated_notes: **Uninsulated sheds and outbuildings** largely follow ambient outdoor conditions. Very wide temperature bands are acceptable (summer up to 32 °C, winter down to −15 °C in Scandinavia). Primary concerns are high humidity (mould on stored items) and condensation on cold metal surfaces during day/night swings. Monitor RH closely – keep below 80 % most days to minimise mould risk.
scenarios.sc_cinema_theater_desc: High peak occupancy: CO₂ and temperature rise fast. EN 16798-1 Cat. II.
scenarios.sc_cinema_theater_notes: **Cinemas and theatres** experience CO₂ peaks at full occupancy – limit set at 1000 ppm. Very low noise limit (40 dB) as background noise directly disturbs the experience. Temperature 22–26 °C and humidity 35–65 % ensure audience comfort.
scenarios.sc_swimming_pool_desc: EN 15288 / VDI 2089: air temp 2–4 °C above water temp. 28–32 °C, RH 50–65 %.
scenarios.sc_swimming_pool_notes: **Swimming pools** require air temperature 28–32 °C (2–4 °C above water) per EN 15288 / VDI 2089. Humidity 50–65 % is critical to limit condensation on building components and chloramine evaporation causing respiratory irritation.
scenarios.sc_hotel_room_desc: EN 16798-1 Cat. I (high expectation): 21–25 °C, RH 30–60 %. Low noise.
scenarios.sc_hotel_room_notes: **Hotel rooms** follow EN 16798-1 Category I (high expectation): 22–26 °C summer. CO₂ max 800 ppm and VOC max 200 ppb ensure good air quality. Low noise limit (35 dB) is crucial for guest sleep comfort and satisfaction.
scenarios.sc_art_gallery_desc: ASHRAE Class B / Bizot Group: 19–23 °C, RH 40–55 %. RH stability more important than absolute value.
scenarios.sc_art_gallery_notes: **Art galleries** follow ASHRAE Class B / Bizot Group: 19–23 °C and 42–55 % RH. RH stability matters more than absolute values – daily fluctuations should stay within ±5 %. Low VOC (150 ppb) protects surfaces and pigments.
scenarios.sc_data_center_desc: ASHRAE TC 9.9 A1: 18–27 °C, RH 8–80 %. Recommended: 18–25 °C, RH 20–60 %.
scenarios.sc_data_center_notes: **Data centres** follow ASHRAE TC 9.9 (2021): recommended 18–25 °C, dew point ≤15 °C. Humidity 20–60 % prevents static discharge (low RH) and condensation (high RH). Higher noise tolerance (70 dB) due to cooling systems.
scenarios.sc_bakery_production_desc: High heat from ovens. Proofing: 24–32 °C, RH 75–85 %. General production: 20–30 °C.
scenarios.sc_bakery_production_notes: **Bakery production** has a wide temperature range (20–30 °C) due to oven heat. Higher humidity (40–75 %) is necessary for dough proofing. CO₂ up to 1500 ppm and VOC up to 400 ppb are expected from fermentation and baking processes.

alarms.submit_error: Could not acknowledge the alarm — please try again.

alarms.attachments_compression_failed: Could not process the photo — try a different one.
alarms.attachments_permission_denied: Camera access was denied. Enable it in Settings to take photos.
